findings.title: nginx.conf has no HTTPS server block despite port 443 being published in docker-compose.prod.yml
findings.description: The nginx configuration defines only a single HTTP server block listening on port 80. docker-compose.prod.yml publishes port 443 (`"${HTTPS_PORT:-443}:443"`) and mounts certbot certificates (`certbot_certs:/etc/nginx/ssl:ro`), but nginx.conf has no corresponding `listen 443 ssl` block, no TLS directives, no HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect, and no HSTS header. Port 443 in production will silently refuse connections (TCP RST or hang) while all traffic runs over plaintext HTTP, rendering in-transit encryption entirely absent.
findings.root_cause: The nginx.conf was written with a comment stating 'behind host nginx SSL proxy', but TLS termination is never actually configured either in this file or verifiably in any other shipped artifact.
findings.impact: All production traffic (credentials, JWT tokens, session data, PII) is transmitted in plaintext. An on-path attacker can intercept or modify any request/response. The certbot certificate provisioned via ACME is never used.
acceptance_criteria: nginx -t passes with HTTPS server block
HTTP requests to port 80 return 301 redirect to https://
curl -I https://orchestr-a.com returns 200 with Strict-Transport-Security header
findings.verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-nginx-prod nginx -t && curl -I https://orchestr-a.com 2>&1 | grep -E 'HTTP|Strict-Transport'
findings.notes: The `certbot_certs` volume and port 443 binding in docker-compose.prod.yml demonstrate the intent for HTTPS, but the nginx.conf was never completed. This is not a 'behind host proxy' configuration — no host proxy artifact is included in the repo.

findings.title: No Strict-Transport-Security header emitted by nginx, web middleware, or API helmet
findings.description: The server block in nginx.conf emits five security headers but omits Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS). The web middleware (apps/web/middleware.ts) emits only CSP. The API helmet configuration in main.ts does not explicitly set HSTS options. Without HSTS, browsers will not upgrade future HTTP requests to HTTPS automatically, leaving users vulnerable to SSL-stripping attacks even after the TLS block is added (finding INFRA-1).
findings.root_cause: HSTS was not included in the security headers block when the nginx config was authored.
findings.impact: After adding TLS (INFRA-1 fix), users who first visit via HTTP are still vulnerable to SSL-stripping for the lifetime of that session. Preload list submission is blocked without a valid HSTS header.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `add_header Strict-Transport-Security 'max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload' always;` to the HTTPS server block in nginx.conf. Do NOT set it on the HTTP-only block (would violate RFC 6797 requirement that HSTS only be sent over TLS). Optionally add it to the helmet configuration in main.ts with `{ strictTransportSecurity: { maxAge: 63072000, includeSubDomains: true, preload: true } }`.
acceptance_criteria: curl -I https://orchestr-a.com returns Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload
HTTP-only responses do not include HSTS header

findings.title: nginx forwards client-supplied X-Forwarded-Proto ($http_x_forwarded_proto) instead of $scheme, enabling proto spoofing
findings.description: Both the /api and / location blocks forward the X-Forwarded-Proto header using `$http_x_forwarded_proto` — the value of the incoming request's header — rather than `$scheme` (nginx's own connection scheme). If this nginx instance is the edge proxy (or if the upstream host-level proxy doesn't strip the header), a client can send `X-Forwarded-Proto: https` on an HTTP request and the backend will believe the connection is HTTPS. This can bypass HTTPS-required guards, influence redirect generation, and affect cookie Secure flag logic based on the detected protocol.
findings.root_cause: The variable `$http_x_forwarded_proto` reflects the client-supplied header value without sanitization; `$scheme` would reflect the actual nginx-to-backend transport (and the outer proxy, if real, should set the canonical value before forwarding).
findings.impact: A client on HTTP can claim to be on HTTPS. Any backend logic gating on `X-Forwarded-Proto` (e.g., cookie Secure enforcement, HTTPS-only redirects) can be bypassed. Combined with the absence of TLS (INFRA-1), this means the API cannot distinguish HTTP from HTTPS at all.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace `$http_x_forwarded_proto` with `$scheme` in both location blocks:
```nginx
proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
```
If there IS a host-level proxy doing TLS termination and setting X-Forwarded-Proto, the inner nginx should OVERWRITE the header with `$scheme` (the value seen by inner nginx), not blindly forward what the outer chain sent — the correct value will be set by the TLS-terminating proxy upstream.
acceptance_criteria: curl -H 'X-Forwarded-Proto: https' http://orchestr-a.com/api/health does not result in backend receiving X-Forwarded-Proto: https when the actual connection is HTTP
nginx config uses $scheme for X-Forwarded-Proto on both /api and / locations
findings.notes: The comment says 'behind host nginx SSL proxy', but the shipped repo contains no host-proxy config. Even if that proxy exists, the inner nginx should use $scheme or a statically set value, not blindly forward client headers.

findings.title: Swagger Basic Auth: passwords containing ':' are silently truncated, enabling credential bypass with a shorter guessable suffix
findings.description: The Basic Auth credential parser uses `.split(':')` without a limit argument. If the configured SWAGGER_PASS contains a colon (e.g. `p@ss:word`), destructuring assignment `const [user, pass]` will receive only `p@ss` as `pass`, silently discarding the `word` suffix. The safeEqual comparison then compares the truncated string against the full configured password — which will fail — locking out the legitimate user. Conversely, an attacker who knows the password up to the first colon can log in with `p@ss` as the password. The HTTP Basic Auth RFC 7617 specifies that only the FIRST colon is a separator; the rest of the decoded string is the password.
findings.root_cause: `.split(':')` without a count/limit parameter splits on ALL colons; the RFC-correct approach is to split on the FIRST colon only.
findings.impact: If SWAGGER_PASS contains a colon, the effective password is only the substring before the first colon. An attacker knowing the password structure can authenticate with a shorter value. Conversely, a legitimately chosen strong password containing ':' silently becomes weaker.
acceptance_criteria: Swagger Basic Auth correctly authenticates when SWAGGER_PASS='p@ss:word' and the request uses password 'p@ss:word'
Swagger Basic Auth rejects requests using only 'p@ss' as password when SWAGGER_PASS='p@ss:word'
findings.verification_command: N/A — manual verification: inspect main.ts line 198-200
findings.notes: This only affects deployments where SWAGGER_ENABLED=true and SWAGGER_PASS contains a colon. Low probability in practice, but the parser is incorrect per RFC 7617.

findings.subcategory: Missing AUDIT_HASH_KEY in CI — E2E API startup will crash
findings.title: CI e2e-smoke and e2e-tests jobs start the API without AUDIT_HASH_KEY, causing assertAuditHashKey() to abort in all environments
findings.code_evidence:
- name: Start Backend API
        run: |
          pnpm --filter api start > /tmp/api.log 2>&1 &
          echo "Waiting for API to be ready..."
          timeout 60 bash -c 'until curl -f http://localhost:4000/api/health 2>/dev/null; do sleep 2; done'
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://orchestr_a:orchestr_a_dev_password@localhost:5432/orchestr_a_v2_e2e
          JWT_SECRET: test-jwt-secret-key
          REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379
          PORT: 4000
          ALLOWED_ORIGINS: http://localhost:3000
          TZ: Europe/Paris
findings.description: Both `e2e-smoke` and `e2e-tests` CI jobs start the API server without setting the `AUDIT_HASH_KEY` environment variable. The `assertAuditHashKey()` function in main.ts is enforced in ALL environments (not just production) and will throw an Error if `AUDIT_HASH_KEY` is absent or shorter than 32 characters. This means both E2E jobs fail silently (the API crashes immediately, the `curl` healthcheck loop times out after 60 seconds, and E2E tests never run). The security gate — correct — but its absence from CI blocks E2E test coverage.
findings.root_cause: The AUDIT_HASH_KEY requirement was added as a universal (non-prod-gated) boot assertion, but the CI workflow was not updated to provide the variable in E2E startup steps.
findings.impact: All E2E tests in CI are silently skipped/fail because the API never starts. Security coverage from E2E tests is absent. If CI is mis-read as passing (e.g., due to `timeout` exit code handling), regressions go undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `AUDIT_HASH_KEY: a-32-char-or-longer-test-audit-hash-key` to the `env:` block of every 'Start Backend API' step in ci.yml (both e2e-smoke and e2e-tests jobs). Use a 32+ character test value; it is not a secret in this context (it protects the audit trail HMAC, and the test DB is ephemeral).
acceptance_criteria: Both e2e-smoke and e2e-tests jobs in CI successfully start the API (healthcheck passes within 60s)
AUDIT_HASH_KEY is present and >= 32 chars in all 'Start Backend API' env blocks
findings.notes: This is simultaneously a CI reliability issue and a security coverage gap — E2E security tests cannot run when the API crashes on boot.

findings.title: nginx sets X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN but CSP sets frame-ancestors: 'none' — conflicting framing policies
findings.description: The nginx server block emits `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN` for the `/` (frontend) location. The Next.js CSP middleware (`apps/web/src/lib/csp.ts`) emits `frame-ancestors 'none'` in the Content-Security-Policy response header. Modern browsers honor `frame-ancestors` (CSP level 2) over `X-Frame-Options` when both are present, so the effective policy is `frame-ancestors 'none'` (no embedding allowed). However, `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN` remains in the response and contradicts the CSP. This creates auditor confusion, may cause issues with legacy browsers that only honor XFO, and the two headers together could interact unexpectedly with some proxy/WAF configurations.
findings.root_cause: The nginx headers were authored before or independently of the CSP middleware implementation, and the two were never reconciled.
findings.impact: Minor: effective policy in modern browsers is the stricter `frame-ancestors 'none'` (good). In legacy browsers that only respect XFO, the policy is `SAMEORIGIN` (weaker than intended). Auditors may flag the inconsistency.
findings.suggested_fix: Align the policies. If the intent is 'no framing' (as expressed by `frame-ancestors 'none'`), change nginx to `add_header X-Frame-Options "DENY" always;` or remove it entirely (since frame-ancestors supersedes XFO in modern browsers). Do NOT change the CSP middleware.
acceptance_criteria: X-Frame-Options value in nginx is either DENY or absent
CSP frame-ancestors: 'none' remains in the web middleware
findings.notes: The `/_next/static` location block also sets `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN` (line 161) — same issue applies there.

findings.title: Dev docker-compose.yml exposes Redis (6379) and PostgreSQL (5432) on all host interfaces without authentication
findings.code_evidence:
redis:
    image: redis:7.4-alpine
    container_name: orchestr-a-redis
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "${REDIS_PORT:-6379}:6379"
findings.description: The development docker-compose.yml binds both PostgreSQL (port 5432) and Redis (port 6379) to all host interfaces (`0.0.0.0`) via the `ports:` directive. Redis has no password (`requirepass`) configured. PostgreSQL uses a well-known default password (`orchestr_a_dev_password`, hardcoded in docker-compose.yml and ci.yml). On a developer machine connected to a network, both services are reachable by anyone on that network segment without authentication.
findings.root_cause: Default development configuration prioritizes convenience (no auth, exposed ports) without restricting binding to loopback.
findings.impact: On shared networks (office Wi-Fi, CI runners with misconfigured network isolation), any host can read/write the development database and cache. CI runner PostgreSQL is also exposed (ci.yml publishes port 5432 with default password). Risk is limited to development/CI environments.
findings.suggested_fix: Bind to loopback only: change `ports: - "${REDIS_PORT:-6379}:6379"` to `ports: - "127.0.0.1:${REDIS_PORT:-6379}:6379"` and similarly for PostgreSQL. Alternatively use `expose:` (internal only) and rely on the docker network. Add a Redis password for dev parity with prod: `command: redis-server --requirepass ${REDIS_PASSWORD:-dev_password}`.
acceptance_criteria: netstat/ss on the dev host shows Redis and PostgreSQL bound to 127.0.0.1, not 0.0.0.0
An external host on the same network cannot connect to port 5432 or 6379
findings.notes: This is a dev-only concern. The prod compose correctly uses `expose:` (not `ports:`) for database and Redis.

findings.title: nginx rate limiting applies only to /api — frontend / and /_next/static locations are unthrottled
findings.description: The nginx `limit_req` and `limit_conn` directives are defined for the `/api` location block only (lines 121-122). The frontend (`location /`) and static asset (`location /_next/static`) locations have no rate limiting. An attacker can flood the Next.js web process with unbounded requests. While Next.js itself is statically served for most pages, SSR routes and API proxy calls are still processed per-request by the web container.
findings.root_cause: Rate limiting was scoped to the API backend only, under the assumption that the frontend is static. Next.js SSR routes are not static and can consume significant server resources per request.
findings.impact: DoS via resource exhaustion of the web container is possible without any throttle. This is less severe than an API-layer DoS because there are no database queries on pure static pages, but SSR pages involve full rendering.
acceptance_criteria: nginx.conf defines a rate limit zone for the web frontend
location / applies limit_req from that zone

verified_clean: apps/api/src/main.ts: ValidationPipe with whitelist:true, forbidNonWhitelisted:true enforced globally
apps/api/src/main.ts: RBAC_GUARD_MODE=permissive blocked in production (SEC-001)
apps/api/src/main.ts: JWT_SECRET strength enforced in production via assertJwtSecretStrength (SEC-026)
apps/api/src/main.ts: AUTH_EXPOSE_RESET_TOKEN=true blocked in production (SEC-018)
apps/api/src/main.ts: AUDIT_HASH_KEY enforced in all environments (OBS-028)
apps/api/src/main.ts: CORS configured with explicit origin allowlist via resolveAllowedOrigins()
apps/api/src/main.ts: Swagger protected with HTTP Basic Auth using timing-safe comparison (timingSafeEqual)
apps/api/src/main.ts: Swagger enabled in prod requires SWAGGER_USER and SWAGGER_PASS
apps/api/src/main.ts: bodyLimit 1MiB set on Fastify adapter (SEC-025)
apps/api/src/main.ts: @fastify/helmet registered with CSP directives for the API
apps/api/src/main.ts: File upload size limited to 2MiB via fastifyMultipart limits
apps/api/src/main.ts: /api/uploads/ requires valid Bearer JWT via createUploadsAuthHook (SEC-016)
apps/api/src/common/fastify/cors.config.ts: production requires CORS_ORIGIN or ALLOWED_ORIGINS, throws on absence
apps/api/src/common/fastify/redact.config.ts: authorization, cookie, password, token fields redacted from Fastify logs
apps/api/src/common/fastify/trust-proxy.config.ts: trustProxy scoped to loopback+uniquelocal only (SEC-013), prevents IP spoofing from public networks
apps/api/src/common/fastify/request-id.context.ts: x-request-id header sanitized (CR/LF stripped, capped at 128 chars) to prevent log injection
apps/api/src/common/fastify/uploads-auth.hook.ts: OPTIONS preflight correctly exempted, actual GET/HEAD requires Bearer token
apps/api/src/common/filters/all-exceptions.filter.ts: non-HttpException errors return opaque 500 — no stack/message leakage to client
apps/api/src/common/guards/ownership.guard.ts: ownership checked server-side via DB query; bypassPermission uses permission system not hardcoded roles
apps/api/src/common/services/access-scope.service.ts: project/task/document/user read scopes use parameterized Prisma queries, no raw SQL
apps/api/src/common/services/ownership.service.ts: all resource ownership checks use parameterized Prisma findUnique/findFirst queries
apps/api/src/common/services/role-hierarchy.service.ts: role assignment enforcement prevents privilege escalation above caller's template rank
apps/api/src/common/services/cache.service.ts: Redis password loaded from config, not hardcoded; no secrets logged
apps/api/src/common/upload/magic-bytes.validator.ts: file-type library used for magic-byte validation; MIME allowlist enforced; no DB-trusted filename used
apps/api/src/common/validators/password-policy.ts: password complexity regex requires uppercase+digit+special; @IsStrongPassword decorator centralizes policy
apps/api/src/common/config/jwt-secret.ts: JWT_SECRET minimum 32 chars enforced in production
apps/api/src/common/config/audit-hash-key.ts: AUDIT_HASH_KEY minimum 32 chars enforced in all environments
apps/api/src/common/error-reporter.ts: process-level unhandledRejection/uncaughtException handlers installed idempotently; no external egress in noop impl
apps/web/middleware.ts: per-request nonce generated via crypto.randomUUID(), injected into CSP for Next.js hydration scripts (SEC-CSP-001)
apps/web/middleware.ts: CSP only enforced in production; dev mode skips to allow HMR
apps/web/src/lib/csp.ts: script-src uses nonce-only (no unsafe-inline); style-src retains unsafe-inline (lower risk, common framework need)
apps/web/src/lib/csp.ts: frame-ancestors 'none' prevents clickjacking
docker-compose.prod.yml: DATABASE_PASSWORD, REDIS_PASSWORD, JWT_SECRET, AUDIT_HASH_KEY, APP_DATABASE_PASSWORD all required (:? operator) — no defaults
docker-compose.prod.yml: postgres, redis, api, web all use expose: (not ports:) except nginx — no direct DB/cache access from host
docker-compose.prod.yml: Redis uses requirepass with mandatory REDIS_PASSWORD
docker-compose.prod.yml: PostgreSQL uses scram-sha-256 authentication
docker-compose.prod.yml: RBAC_GUARD_MODE hardcoded to 'enforce' in API environment
docker-compose.prod.yml: api and web containers use cap_drop: ALL and no-new-privileges:true
nginx/nginx.conf: server_tokens off hides nginx version
nginx/nginx.conf: X-Content-Type-Options, X-XSS-Protection, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy headers set
nginx/nginx.conf: CSP intentionally absent from nginx HTML path (correctly delegates to Next.js nonce middleware)
nginx/nginx.conf: API CSP correctly delegated to @fastify/helmet
.github/workflows/ci.yml: no hardcoded production secrets; all prod secrets use GitHub Secrets mechanism
.github/workflows/docker-publish.yml: uses GITHUB_TOKEN for GHCR auth; no hardcoded registry credentials
.github/workflows/backlog-coherence.yml: no secrets; read-only checkout
.github/workflows/permission-matrix-coverage.yml: no secrets; read-only checkout
